nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded forest igapó habitats along the Rio Negro and its tributaries; typically over a substrate of leaf litter, sand, and submerged woody debris beneath dense riparian canopy

decorPreferences: coconut cave or small ceramic cave for spawning
dim lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce)
dense background planting (Java fern, Anubias on wood)
blackwater tannins (peat filtration or alder cones)

diet.notes: Primarily small invertebrates in the wild: microcrustaceans, insect larvae, worms, and zooplankton. In captivity, accepts frozen or live foods such as baby brine shrimp, micro worms, daphnia, Cyclops, and blackworms. High-quality small-pellet or micro-granule cichlid foods may be accepted once acclimated but live and frozen foods are essential for conditioning breeding pairs. Variety and regular small feedings promote best coloration and health.

goodWith: small, non-competitive characins tolerant of acidic water (ember tetras, green neon tetras, pencilfish)
small Corydoras species (sterbai, habrosus) as bottom-level dithers
dwarf shrimp (adults generally safe; fry may be predated)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species or dwarf cichlids (territorial conflict)
any fish requiring alkaline or neutral water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery page for Apistogramma mendezi; individual image licensing varies by contributor. Check page for CC-licensed images.

summary: A rare and demanding Rio Negro blackwater specialist in the pertensis/diplotaenia group. Males develop a striking lyretail caudal fin and intense yellow-and-blue coloration. Strictly requires soft, acidic blackwater; unsuitable for community tanks without careful water chemistry management. Haremic cave-spawner with excellent parental care. Advanced-level species.
careNotes: Apistogramma mendezi is a blackwater obligate from the Rio Negro drainage of Brazil, placing it firmly in the specialist category. This species belongs to the pertensis/diplotaenia species group, characterized by elongated lyretail caudal fins in males, bold lateral stripe patterns, and strong adaptation to extremely soft, acidic water. pH must be maintained between 4.5 and 6.0 — values above 6.5 are associated with poor coloration, increased disease susceptibility, and breeding failure. General hardness should be near zero (0–5 °dGH) and KH essentially nil. Reverse osmosis water remineralized with blackwater extracts (Indian almond leaves, peat, alder cones) is the standard approach; tap water is typically unsuitable unless it is naturally very soft. Temperature 24–29 °C; some sources suggest 26–28 °C as optimal for conditioning. A well-established tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is the minimum recommended for a single pair or a harem group of one male with two females. The substrate should be fine sand at least 3 cm deep; leaf litter is essential both as cover and as a source of tannins and microorganisms. Caves (coconut halves, small ceramic tunnels, or overturned clay pots) are required for spawning and shelter. Avoid strong water flow — still to very gentle circulation mimics the natural igapó environment. Males are territorial and will defend their area aggressively, particularly during spawning; multiple females can be kept in larger tanks (90+ L) with clearly delineated territories. Choose dither fish carefully: only species tolerant of pH 4.5–6.0 are appropriate (ember tetras, green neon tetras, pencilfish, Boraras). Larger tanks allow a harem (1 male : 2–3 females), which reduces male aggression toward individual females.
breedingNotes: A haremic cave-spawner displaying pronounced sexual dimorphism. Males reach 7–8 cm with extended lyretail finnage and vivid yellow and blue patterning; females are smaller (4–5 cm) and yellow when guarding eggs. Conditioning with live and frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro worms) over 2–4 weeks triggers spawning readiness. The female selects and prepares a cave; 40–100 eggs are deposited on the cave ceiling or walls and are guarded by the female with intense dedication. Males are excluded from the immediate spawn site but patrol the outer territory. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 27 °C; fry become free-swimming after a further 4–6 days. The female actively shepherds fry for 2–4 weeks. Feed fry infusoria, paramecia, and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Remove the male once fry are free-swimming if aggression toward the female intensifies in a small tank. Water quality must remain stable throughout: any pH or hardness fluctuation can cause egg abandonment.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma mendezi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Negro, Brazil), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, temperature 24–28°C, softness requirement), adult size, diet, breeding behaviour (cave-spawner, haremic, female egg-guarding), species group placement (pertensis/diplotaenia), and husbandry guidance. Basis for water chemistry, tank setup, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma mendezi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Rio Negro basin, Amazonas state, Brazil), adult size (males to 8 cm SL), temperature range, and species author (Romer, 1994). Confirmed blackwater habitat classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guidance used to cross-check tank size recommendations, diet (live/frozen foods emphasis), haremic grouping rationale, cave-spawning behaviour, and compatibility advice. Corroborated female-guarded spawning and need for soft acidic water.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for species group context (pertensis/diplotaenia group), lyretail male morphology, and advanced care classification. Corroborated blackwater obligate status and RO water requirement for Rio Negro species.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Apistogramma (genus overview)
additional.notes: Genus-level overview used for compatibility, diet breadth, and general Apistogramma breeding protocol. Confirmed haremic structure (1 male : 2–3 females) and cave-spawning with female parental care as typical for genus.

fish.breedingNotes: Haremic cave-spawner; 40–100 eggs deposited in a cave and guarded exclusively by the female. Fry are free-swimming after ~7–10 days. RO-quality blackwater (pH 5.0–5.5, near-zero hardness) essential for successful egg development. Condition with live/frozen foods for 2–4 weeks pre-spawn.
